entries.english: Prabha: Greetings, Sheelaji
Sheela: Greetings, I am fine
Prabha: I am fine
Sheela: Greetings
and
Prabha: Greetings, Sheelaji
Sheela: Greetings
Prabha: I am fine
Sheela: Greetings
Dulari: Greetings
Sheela: Where do you live?
Dulari: We live in Delhi.
Sheela: When did you come to Kashmir?
Dulari: We came yesterday.
Sheela: How long will you stay in Kashmir?
Dulari: We shall stay for a m onth or two.
Sheela: Our home is nearby
Prabha: No, not today
Sheela: Dulariji, you should come too.
Dulari: Yes, I will certainly come with Prabhaji.
Sheela: All right
Dulari and Sheela: Goodbye
A conversation with a coolie
'laborer, coolie' and a tourist
Ashok: Yes, but how much will you charge? Once a price is quoted by the seller, one habitually responds,
Ashok: No, that is too much
is ubiquitous and carries anything from firewood to fruit.
Porter: Do you have any luggage, sir?
Ashok: Yes, two cases and one bed-roll.
Porter: Shall I carry the luggage?
Ashok: Yes, but how much will you charge?
Porter: Where do you have to go? (Where are you going?)
Ashok: I have to go to Dal Gate.
Porter: Only ten rupees.
Ashok: No, that is too much.
Porter: No, sir, this luggage is heavy
Ashok: Can we get another coolie?
Porter: Yes, sir, we are two.
Ashok: All right, say how much you will charge.
Porter: Just ten rupees, sir.
Ashok: No, eight rupees.
Porter: All right, sir, let us go.
Ashok: This is (my) luggage

title: A conversation with a hotel keeper" height="60" width="246">

entries.english: A conversation with a hotel keeper
Hotel Keeper: Greetings, sir.
Ashok: Greetings, do you have a vacant room?
Hotel Keeper: Yes, sir, I do.
Ashok: I want a room.
Hotel Keeper: How many days will you stay?
Ashok: Two days.
Hotel Keeper: All right, please write your name on this register.
Ashok: What is the charge?
Hotel Keeper: Do you want only the room, or room and board?
Ashok: No, just the room.
Hotel Keeper: The room is twenty rupees.
Ashok: All right, that's fine.
Hotel Keeper: Here is the key.
Ashok: What is the room number?
Hotel Keeper: It is number four
Ashok: The luggage is in taxi.
Hotel Keeper: You go to your room
Ashok: Fine, thank you

title: A conversation with a houseboat owner" height="68" width="293">

entries.english: A conversation with a houseboat owner
Houseboat owner: Certainly, take a look at it
Houseboat owner: For how long do you want to stay? (and, the invariable inquiry)
Ashok: What is the charge? It is after this last inquiry that the unavoidable bargaining begins.
Houseboat owner: Greetings, Sahib
Ashok: Well, I would like to see the houseboat.
Houseboat owner: Certainly, take a look at it
Ashok: I am the only one.
Houseboat owner: For how long do you want to stay?
Ashok: One week.
Houseboat owner: All right, let's go.
Ashok: How far is the houseboat?
Houseboat owner: Near Dal Gate.
Ashok: How many rooms does it have?
Houseboat owner: Four rooms, two for sleeping (two bedrooms), one for sitting (one living room), and one for cooking and dining (and a dinette-kitchen).
Ashok: What is the charge?
Houseboat owner: Just twenty rupees a day.
Ashok: All right, I will look at the houseboat.
Houseboat owner: Yes, sir, let's go

title: A conversation with a tonga driver" height="76" width="284">

entries.english: A conversation with a tonga driver
Ashok: Hello, tongawala, will you go to Nehru Park?
Tonga driver: Yes, sir, climb in!
Ashok: How far is Nehru Park from here?
Tonga driver: It will take an hour (to get there).
Ashok: What is the charge?
Tonga driver: Ten rupees.
Ashok: I won't pay more than five rupees.
Tonga driver: No, that's not enough
Ashok: All right, let's go!
Tonga driver: All right

title: A conversation with a boatman" width="256" height="67">

entries.english: A conversation with a boatman
Whatever you like, sir
Ashok: Is this your boat?
Boatman: Yes, sir, what can I do for you?
Ashok: Will you go to Nishat (Garden)?
Boatman: Yes, come on down (here).
Ashok: How long will it take? Boatman: One hour
Boatman: Whatever you like, sir.
Ashok: No, (tell me) what is the rate?
Boatman: Eight rupees.
Ashok: No, six rupees is a fair price.
Boatman: All right, come on down.
Ashok: All right, let's go

title: A conversation with a taxi driver" width="267" height="65">

entries.english: A conversation with a taxi driver
Ashok: Is the taxi available (for hire)?
Taxi driver: Yes, (please) get in
Ashok: I want to go to the post office.
Taxi driver: Which post office?
Ashok: The head post office.
Taxi driver: All right, come on in.
Ashok: How far is it?
Taxi driver: It is two miles from here.
Ashok: All right, (please) hurry.
Taxi driver: All right, sir, (we're) on our way

title: A conversation with a tea seller" width="264" height="60">

entries.english: A conversation with a tea seller
Ashok: Give me a cup of tea, please.
Tea seller: Please sit down
Ashok: Lipton.
Tea seller: Shall I add sugar and milk?
Ashok: Yes, but only a little.
Tea seller: Here you are.
Ashok: How much do I pay?
Tea seller: Just fifty paise.
Ashok: Here, take this one-rupee bill (note).
Tea seller: Here is your change.
Ashok: Thank you
A conversation with a milkman
Ashok: How much is a kilo of milk?
Milkman: Two rupees, sir.
Ashok: Milk or yogurt?
Milkman: Yes, milk and yogurt both sell for two rupees a kilo.
Ashok: What price is the cheese?
Milkman: Five rupees a kilo.
Ashok: Do you have home delivery of milk?
Milkman: Yes, every morning
Ashok: In the white house, right here near the bend.
Milkman: We'll deliver it for you
Ashok: One kilo of milk and one kilo of yogurt.
Milkman: Very well, sir.
Ashok: Do you want a deposit?
Milkman: No, we shall keep a monthly account and send you the bill each month.
Ashok: Fine, thank you

title: A conversation with a fruit seller" width="273" height="63">

entries.english: A conversation with a fruit seller
Ashok: What is this fruit?
Fruit seller: These are stunted apples.
Ashok: Fine! Are these cherries?
Fruit seller: Yes, there are
cherries.
Ashok: How much are the stunted apples?
Fruit seller: The stunted apples are two rupees a kilo.
Ashok: How much are the
cherries?
Fruit seller: The
cherries are two rupees a kilo.
Ashok: Do you have (any) apples?
Fruit seller: Yes, sir, in that basket
Ashok: Are they any good?
Fruit seller: Yes, these are amber apples.
Ashok: How much are the pears?
Fruit seller: Pears are four for a rupee.
Ashok: Very well, give me one kilo of
apples, one kilo of
cherries, and eight pears.
Fruit seller: Do you have a basket?
Ashok: No, please give (them to me) in a paper sack.
Fruit seller: All right.
Fruit seller: The total bill is seven rupees.
Ashok: Here is a ten rupee bill.
Fruit seller: Here is your change.
Ashok: Thank you

title: A conversation with a vegetable seller

entries.english: A conversation with a vegetable seller
Vegetable seller: What would you like, sir?
Ashok: Are there any fresh vegetables?
Vegetable seller: Everything is fresh
Ashok: What is the price of spinach and potatoes?
Vegetable seller: Spinach is one rupee a kilo
Ashok: How much are radishes and eggplants?
Vegetable seller: A bunch of radishes is ten paise, and each eggplant is six paise.
Ashok: Are cucumbers also sold by the kilo?
Vegetable seller: No, sir, cucumbers are twenty paise each.
Ashok: All right, give me one kilo of potatoes, two kilos of spinach, and four bunches of radishes.
Vegetable seller: All right, (will there be) anything else?
Ashok: No, here is your money.
Vegetable seller: Thank you

title: A conversation with a sweetmeat seller" width="269" height="63">

entries.english: A conversation with a sweetmeat seller
Ashok: What kind of sweetmeats are on this plate?
Sweetmeat seller: These are
Ashok: How much do these
cost?
Sweetmeat seller: They are six rupees a kilo.
Ashok: I see
Sweetmeat seller: Yes,
are sold by the kilo.
Ashok: What is on these plates?
Sweetmeat seller: This is coconut
and these are
Ashok: What has been pasted on the top of these sweetmeats?
Sweetmeat seller: These are silver leaves and gold leaves.
Ashok: Does one eat them?
Sweetmeat seller: Yes, of course.
Ashok: Did you sell all these sweets by the kilo?
Sweetmeat seller: No, we sell
and
by the dozen.
Ashok: How much are they per dozen?
Sweetmeat seller: Both are four rupees a dozen.
Ashok: How much are
and
Sweetmeat seller: Eight rupees a kilo.
Ashok: Give me half a kilo of gulab
and half a kilo of
Sweetmeat seller: Anything else?
Ashok: Well yes, also give me six
and six
Sweetmeat seller: Here they are.
Ashok: How much is it?
Sweetmeat seller: The total is seven rupees.
Ashok: Here take this ten rupees bill.
Sweetmeat seller: This is your change.
Ashok: Thank you
A conversation with a butcher
Butcher: What would you like, sir?
Ashok: I want one kilo of meat
Butcher: (Do you want it) with intestines or without intestines?
Ashok: (No) Without intestines.
Butcher: It is six rupees a kilo without intestines.
Ashok: All right, give me one kilo.
Butcher: What else, sir?
Ashok: I also want one kilo of ground meat and half a kilo of liver.
Butcher: All right, I will prepare the ground meat.
Ashok: Are liver and ground meat both six rupees a kilo?
Butcher: No, sir, liver is six rupees a kilo, but the ground meat is eight rupees a kilo.
Ashok: I see, the ground meat is more expensive than liver!
Butcher: But this is good quality ground meat.
Ashok: What is the total bill?
Butcher: The total bill is seventeen rupees
Ashok: Wrap it up (in paper)
Butcher: All right.
Ashok: Here is your money.
Butcher: Thank you
The people of Kashmir
and saree
is a g.mp3ent worn in Kashmir.
Ashok: Greetings, Mohanji.
Mohan: Greetings, Ashok
Ashok: I am fine
Mohan: Really? That is good.
Ashok: It is not a particularly good book
Mohan: Yes, certainly.
Ashok: What is the religion of Kashmiris?
Mohan: Kashmiris are not of one religion
Ashok: Are there really four religions in Kashmir?
Mohan: Yes, of course!
Ashok: But are they mostly Muslims?
Mohan: Yes, they are mostly Muslims.
Ashok: What are the festivals of Kashmir?
Mohan: Kashmiri Muslims celebrate Ramdan
and
Ashok: Do the Kashmiris wear the
Mohan: Yes, they do.
Ashok: What do the women wear?
Mohan: In Kashmir women wear the
or the
and the Hindu women also wear the saree.
Ashok: The phiran is a very attractive dress.
Mohan: Yes, and w.mp3 too.
Ashok: Thank you
Mohan: All right, let's meet again tomorrow.
Ashok: Yes, certainly
Mohan: Goodbye
Kashmiri food
Ashok: Greetings, Mohan
Mohan: Greetings, Ashok
Ashok: Today I have (some) questions about Kashmiri food.
Mohan: Yes, certainly.
Ashok: What is the (typical) food of Kashmiris?
Mohan: The Kashmiris eat rice both in the morning and in the evening
Ashok: Does paddy grow in Kashmir?
Mohan: Yes, of course.
Ashok: Do people in Kashmir eat meat?
Mohan: Yes, in Kashmir, both Muslims and Hindus eat meat.
Ashok: Tell me, is meat expensive?
Mohan: Yes, meat is more expensive than vegetables.
Ashok: Which vegetables of Kashmir are well-known.
Mohan: Kashmiris eat many kinds of vegetables, but the famous ones are
lotus stem, and eggplant.
Ashok: Just
lotus stem, and eggplant?
Mohan: Not only these, but kohlrabi and turnips as well.
Ashok: One day I must eat kohlrabi and turnips.
Mohan: Kashmiris eat dal also.
Ashok: How do Kashmiris cook meat?
Mohan: Well, do you not eat
(a red-colored spicy meat dish),
(meat cooked with yogurt),
(meat balls cooked in milk - a delicacy of Kashmiri Muslims), or
(minced (ground) meat)?
Ashok: Yes, I do
Mohan: One cook makes very good
(Why don't you) come sometime, and see (how he does it).
Ashok: Is there fish in Kashmir?
Mohan: Yes, the river Vitasta (Jehlum) has several types of fish
Ashok: Do you know how to cook fish?
Mohan: Do not worry
Ashok: Very well, then I will certainly come
Mohan: Yes, (you) certainly (do)!
Ashok: All right
Mohan: Goodbye

title: On sight-seeing in Kashmir" height="53" width="161">

entries.english: On sight-seeing in Kashmir
Ashok: Greetings, Mohan.
Mohan: Greetings, what's new?
Ashok: I am thinking of going sight-seeing outside Srinagar. (Please) tell me how I should go about it.
Mohan: Do you like horseback riding, boating, or hiking?
Ashok: I like both horseback riding and boating.
Mohan: How many weeks do you want to spend sight-seeing?
Ashok: On or two weeks.
Mohan: Very well, for horseback riding you go to Gulmarg and Pahalgam
Ashok: How far is Wular Lake?
Mohan: It should be about thirty miles from Srinagar
Ashok: Where does one get the bus or taxi for sight-seeing?
Mohan: You can make all the (necessary) arrangements at the Tourist Reception Center or at Amira Kadal.
Ashok: Does one also get literature (books) about these places at the Tourist Center?
Mohan: Yes, of course, everything.
Ashok: All right
Mohan: Goodbye
Going on a boat ride
Ashok: Greetings, Mohan.
Mohan: How are things?
Ashok: Fine! I came today to talk with you about sight-seeing in a boat
Mohan: Well, that is not difficult
Ashok: Yes, I know that.
Mohan: There are shikaras and houseboats in Dal Lake
Ashok: What is a bahats?
Mohan: A bahats is a very big boat in whichfirewood, paddy, and other loads are carried
Ashok: What is in Dal Lake?
Mohan: There are beautiful shikaras on Dal Lake
Ashok: How much will it cost?
Mohan: Just five or six rupees.
Ashok: Five or six rupees is not very much.
Mohan: But sight-seeing on a boat on Vitasta River is expensive.
Ashok: Why?
Mohan: It is a long distance from Amira Kadal (bridge) to the Weir
Ashok: How long will it take?
Mohan: Just four or five hours.
Ashok: Where else is sight-seeing by boat possible?
Mohan: You (can) go from Srinagar to Wular Lake
Ashok: Is Wular Lake a big lake?
Mohan: Yes, it is the second largest lake in Asia.
Ashok: How big is Wular Lake?
Mohan: It is spread over twelve and a half square miles.
Ashok: Well, I must go sight-seeing by a boat in Kashmir.
Mohan: Yes, of course.
Ashok: Thank you
Mohan: All right, goodbye.
Ashok: Goodbye
A Kashmiri fire-pot
(shopkeeper) about the
the traditional Kashmiri fire-pot
(a bride's fire-pot), and
(children's fire-pot).
Ashok: What is this?
Shopkeeper: It is called a
Ashok: Say that again (please).
Shopkeeper:
Ashok: What is it used for?
Shopkeeper: One puts live charcoal in the
Ashok: Really? Does one w.mp3 one's body with the
Shopkeeper: Yes, sir.
Ashok: How does one do that?
Shopkeeper: The
is put inside the phiran or in a blanket.
Ashok: The
must cause burns.
Shopkeeper: Yes, sometimes the
does cause burns.
Ashok: Is there only one type of
Shopkeeper: No, sir, there are many types of fire-pots.
Ashok: (Please) show me two or three other types.
Shopkeeper: Look at this one
Ashok: (Yes) This
is very pretty.
Shopkeeper: Yes, sir, a lot of work has been done on this.
Ashok: How much does it cost?
Shopkeeper: It costs fifteen rupees.
Ashok: (Yes) The price is reasonable.
Shopkeeper: There are many other types, too
Ashok: How is the
made?
Shopkeeper: It is made of wicker
Ashok: What is inside?
Shopkeeper: Inside, there is earthen, bowl-like pot in which live charcoal is kept
Ashok: It is a very good way of keeping oneself w.mp3.
Shopkeeper: Yes, that is why Kashmiris like the
very much.
Ashok: All right, give me this
Shopkeeper: Ten rupees.
Ashok: Here is the money

title: Going to Dal Lake" width="157" height="57">

entries.english: Going to Dal Lake
(a boatman) about sight-seeing on and around Dal Lake
Ashok: Hello there, boatman!
Boatman: Yes, sir.
Ashok: How far is Dal Lake from here?
Boatman: This is Habba Kadal
Ashok: We do not have much time
Boatman: If we hurry, it will take us thre to four hours.
Ashok: Can we return before it gets dark?
Boatman: Yes, certainly.
Ashok: All right, what are the things worth seeing at Dal Lake?
Boatman: Dal Lake is very big
Ashok: What is on Dal Lake?
Boatman: In Dal Lake, there are two islets: Golden Island and Silver Island.
Ashok: What are the names of the gardens?
Boatman: The three famous gardens are Nishat, Shalimar, and Chashman Shahi.
Ashok: What is in the gardens?
Boatman: In the gardens there are flower beds, fountains, and beautiful balconies.
Ashok: Are there flowers in Dal Lake?
Boatman: Yes, there are lotuses and vegetables in Dal Lake
Ashok: Can we get something to eat there?
Boatman: Yes, there are many hotels there
(Sama:va:r and Kashmiri Cups) there.
Ashok: All right, let us go then.
Boatman: (Please) come into the boat.
Ashok: How much do I have to pay?
Boatman: Only fifteen rupees.
Ashok: All right, let's go
Visiting Wular Lake
Ashok: Greetings, Mohan
Mohan: Greetings, I'm fine
Ashok: I am thinking of going to Wular lake
Mohan: Before leaving Kashmir, you should definitely go to see Wular Lake.
Ashok: Yes, everybody says the same thing
Mohan: From Srinagar one goes by bus to Sopore, and from Sopore one goes by boat.
Ashok: Is there any other way?
Mohan: Yes, many people go by boat from Srinagar, or (they go) by bus up to Bandipora.
Ashok: What is Wular Lake famous for?
Mohan: Wular Lake is the biggest lake in India
Ashok: How big is it?
Mohan: It is fourteen miles long
Ashok: How many days does it take to visit Wular Lake?
Mohan: If you go by bus or car, it will take you just one day
Ashok: No, I do not have that much time
Mohan: Then you should go by bus.
Ashok: All right, I will do that
Mohan: Goodbye
Going to Gulmarg
Ashok: Greetings, Mohanji.
Mohan: How are you?
Ashok: Yes, all right.
Mohan: How is your sightseeing in Kashmir going?
Ashok: It will take some more time.
Mohan: Where are you going next?
Ashok: I am thinking of going to Gulmarg for two or three days.
Mohan: Yes, why don't you go
Ashok: That is what I have to ask (you)
Mohan: It is very easy to go to Gulmarg
Ashok: What arrangements are there from Tangmarg?
Mohan: From Tangmarg it is three miles to Gulmarg
Ashok: What is there at Gulmarg?
Mohan: Gulmarg is avery attractive spot up in the mountains.
Ashok: Are there trees and flowers at Gulmarg?
Mohan: Yes, Gulmarg is full of deodar trees.
Ashok: Is there some arrangement for tourists to stay there?
Mohan: Yes, there are many hotels and tourist huts there
Ashok: I expect to stay there for two or three days.
Mohan: While there, you will be able to play golf and also go to Khilanmarg.
Ashok: How far is Khilanmarg from Gulmarg?
Mohan: It should be three or four miles (from Gulmarg)
Ashok: All right
Mohan: Goodbye
Visiting Pahalgam
a tourist site about sixty miles from Srinagar
Ashok: Greetings, Mohanji
Mohan: Greetings, I am fine.
Ashok: I came to ask what arrangements I have to make for going to Pahalgam.
Mohan: First, you return from Gulmarg to Srinagar
Ashok: Which way do we (take to) go to Pahalgam?
Mohan: One goes to Pahalgam by an entirely different route.
Ashok: Is Pahalgam prettier than Gulmarg?
Mohan: That is a difficult question (to answer)
Ashok: What is the difference?
Mohan: Pahalgam is at the foot of a mountain
Ashok: Are there (any) hotels there?
Mohan: Oh yes, many
Ashok: Is it possible to go sight-seeing by a boat at Pahalgam?
Mohan: No, boating is not possible because the Lidder is a very fast moving and dangerous stream
Ashok: All right, what places are worth seeing near Pahalgam?
Mohan: On your way to Pahalgam, you will visit Anantnag, Achabal, Kokarnag, and Mattan.
Ashok: Are three or four days enough (for the trip)?
Mohan: Yes, of course.
Ashok: All right, p.mp3it me to leave
Mohan: Goodbye

title: A Kashmiri shawl merchant" width="171" height="53">

entries.english: A Kashmiri shawl merchant
shawls,
shawls,
shawls,
and ring shawls.
Shawl merchant: Greetings, sir
Ashok: I am looking for a Kashmiri shawl and a Kashmiri saree.
Shawl merchant: (Please) have a seat
Ashok: What types of shawls do you have?
Shawl merchant: We have shawls of
and of
Ashok: But pashmina must be very expensive?
Shawl merchant: Yes,
is more expensive than raphal.
Ashok: Is there anything even better than
Shawl merchant: Yes, the
and ring shawls are more expensive than
Ashok: These must be very expensive?
Shawl merchant: Yes, these are more expensive than
Ashok: What is this shawl made of?
Shawl merchant: This shawl is made of
Ashok: This shawl has very fine embroidery on it, but the color is not good
Shawl merchant: Do you like that red shawl over there?
Ashok: I like its color, but the work on it is not good.
Shawl merchant: All right, we will show you another shawl, but its price is a little higher.
Ashok: If the shawl is good, the price does not matter.
Shawl merchant: See what embroidery and what color it has!
Ashok: Yes, this has good embroidery and good color, too
Shawl merchant: This is four yards long.
Ashok: What is its width?
Shawl merchant: Two yards.
Ashok: Is this
or
Shawl merchant: This is very fine
Ashok: How much does this shawl cost?
Shawl merchant: This shawl costs three hundred rupees.
Ashok: All right, I will buy it.
Shawl merchant: Would you like to buy something else?
Ashok: No, not today
Visiting Hazratbal
Ashok: Hello there, taxi driver!
Cab driver: Yes, sir.
Ashok: We want to go to Hazratbal
Cab driver: From here, Hazratbal is about five or six miles.
Ashok: How long will it take?
Cab driver: (It) will take just fifteen or twenty minutes.
Ashok: Where is Hazratbal located?
Cab driver: Hazratbal is close to Nigin Lake near Kashmir University.
Ashok: What is there at Hazratbal?
Cab driver: There is a famous mosque at Hazratbal where Muslims go each Friday to offer prayers
Ashok: Is Hazratbal on the bank of Dal Lake?
Cab driver: No, Hazratbal is on the bank of Nigin Lake.
Ashok: Can one go there only by taxi or tonga?
Cab driver: No, people go there by shikara, too.
Ashok: How long does it take to go there by shikara?
Cab driver: From Amira Kadal, it takes two or three hours.
Ashok: No, we do not have that much time
Cab driver: Please, get in.
Ashok: All right, let's go!
Visiting Tulmul
located near Gandarbal about twenty miles from Srinagar
Mohan: Greetings, Ashok
Ashok: Greetings, Mohan
Mohan: Well, what (places) did you see?
Ashok: Just three or four places
Mohan: Yes, you (must) do that.
Ashok: Yes, that's what I came to talk to you about
from here?
Mohan:
is about fifteen miles away, but it is not difficult to get there
Ashok: Can one return the same day, or does one stay there overnight?
Mohan: Yes, many people go there in the morning and return in the evening
Ashok: Tell me, what is
famous for?
Mohan: I will tell you
there, in the middle of a spring.
Ashok: Do people worship this idol?
Mohan: Yes, the Hindus worship this idol
) into it, and light a
Ashok: Do only Kashmiri Pandits go to
Mohan: Oh no, Hindus and Sikhs come there from every part of India
Ashok: Do the pilgrims take a bath before entering (the shrine)?
Mohan: Yes, near the gate everybody has to take a bath
Ashok: Well, is there any arrangement for food there?
Mohan: Yes, there are many
(sweetmeat sellers) shops
rice pudding, and cooked rice and vegetables
Ashok: On the way to
is there any other place worth seeing?
Mohan: Yes, there are two or three very nice places.
Ashok: Which are they?
Mohan: The big mosque is very near here
Ashok: What is there at Vicharnag and Gandarbal?
Mohan: Vicharnag is a place of pilgrimage for the Hindus, and there is a spring there
Ashok: All right, I shall meet you after returning from
Mohan: Goodbye
Visiting the Amarnath Cave
(see Notes)
'thousands' is introduced
'to have to' and
'to enjoy' appear
Ashok: Hello, Mohan
Mohan: Hello. (Yes) I am fine
Ashok: I came yesterday
Mohan: Amarnath is about eighty miles from Srinagar
Ashok: How does one go there?
Mohan: One goes by bus up to Pahalgam, and from Pahalgam you may go on horseback or on a stretcher.
Ashok: How many people go there?
Mohan: Oh, thousands
Ashok: What kind of arrangements are there?
Mohan: Everything, of course: doctors, medicine, food, clothing, and police.
Ashok: What is the road to Amarnath like?
Mohan: Up to Pahalgam, the road is good
Ashok: That means the journey is difficult?
Mohan: Yes, very difficult
Ashok: At what elevation is Amarnath located?
Mohan: About thirteen thousand feet.
Ashok: What is there on the mountain top?
Mohan: Amarnath is a place for pilgrimage
Ashok: What is inside the cave?
Mohan: It is said that on
a lingam of (Lord) Shiva, made of snow, appears in the cave
Ashok: It must be a beautiful place
Mohan: It takes about four to five days.
Ashok: Perhaps I will go to Amarnath.
Mohan: Oh yes, you should go
Ashok: All right, goodbye
Mohan: Yes, by all means

Lake Dal
Dal Lake is one of the famous lakes in India
), extends from the Dal Gate to what is known as "the camels' grave" (

In Dal Lake there is a spot which is called
Dal Lake is full of houseboats near Dal Gate

title: The Gardens of Kashmir" height="55" width="177">

entries.english: The Gardens of Kashmir
The Gardens of Kashmir
The gardens of Kashmir are famous throughout India
It is said that the Mughal kings Shahjahan and Jahangir were very fond of flower gardens
When you visit these gardens you will see not only flowers, but also various types of trees and fountains
Mahadev Bishta: A Clever Thief
Mahadev Bishta: A Clever Thief
Kashmiri mothers often tell their children the stories of Mahadev at bedtime
" - a sound made to scare away cats - while Mahadev took off with the loot.
It is indeed a fact that Mahadev was a well-known thief
There is a particularly well-known story about Mahadev
One day Mahadev was invited to a gathering of all the thieves
After this it took Mahadev four or five days to think
First, Mahadev went out and filled a piece of reed with vicious red ants
(loincloth) and looked at himself in the mirror
Maharaja was sound asleep
The next day Mahadev went to the gathering of the thieves with the Maharaja's trousers
with a smile
which entertain the Kashmiri children

title: Badshah: The Great King" height="59" width="175">

entries.english: Badshah: The Great King Badshah: The Great King I
Kashmiris have rarely seen good days
Kashmir has been ruled by the Mughals, the Afghans, the Sikhs and the Dogras
But not all the rulers were bad
Gulmarg
Gulmarg
Gulmarg is about twenty-eight miles from Srinagar
' means 'flower' and '
' means 'meadow'
It is situated at a height of eight thousand feet above the sea
Many tourists camp in tents at Gulmarg
The climate of Gulmarg is very good
People play various sports at Gulmarg
There are provisions for everything at Gulmarg
In Gulmarg, the scent of deodar trees comes to you from all around
Kashmiri Saffron Fields
Kashmiri Saffron Fields
In the whole world, there are only two or three places where saffron grows
About nine miles from Srinagar, there is a place called Pampur
Saffron plants are about three inches high
Saffron flowers look very attractive in the fields
), people visit Pampur to enjoy (the beauty of) the saffron fields.
Saffron does not only add to the ch.mp3 of Kashmir, but is also monetarily profitable
There are several uses for saffron inside and outside Kashmir

Kashmiri Samovar
Kashmiri Samovar
There is no home in Kashmir that does not have a samovar
Inside a samovar there is a fire-container in which charcoal and live coals are placed
Samovars are not of only one type
Kashmiris can make two or three types of tea in the samovar
is the favorite tea of Kashmiris
sugar, cardamom, cinnamon, and almonds are boiled in water, but no milk is added.
The second type of tea is
sugar, cardamom, and almonds
at weddings and on feasts.
The third type or tea is called
also is a typically Kashmiri tea, but not everyone likes it.
Recently, some Kashmiris have started drinking Lipton tea
cannot be made without the samovar.
It is difficult to say when the samovar was first introduced into Kashmir
(cup)

title: The Muslim rishis: Nandarishi" height="59" width="231">

entries.english: The Muslim rishis: Nandarishi The Muslim rishis: Nandarishi
For centuries the Hindus and Muslims in Kashmir have lived together
How Sufism came to Kashmir is a long story
Among the "Muslim rishis", the most famous rishi is Sheikh-nur-ud-din
(tsrarishariph)
It is difficult to say when Nandrishi was actually born
Many stories are also told about Nandrishi's parents
Many Kashmiri Hindus and Muslims became Nandrishi's disciples
and devotedly go there to place flowers on the graves
Granny Lalla
Kashmir has produced many saints, poets and mystics
or a devotee of
Kashmiri Hindus and Muslims affectionately call her "Mother Lalla" or "Granny Lalla"
It is said that Lal Ded was born in 1355 in Pandrethan to a Kashmiri Pandit family
When Lalla was twenty-six she renounced the family and became a devotee of Shiva
Why is Lalla so famous in Kashmir? She was illiterate, but she was wise
and
The exact date of Lalla's death is not known
The sayings of Lalla number around two hundred
Habba Khatun
(16th Century) (1885-1952) Habba Khatun
Lal Ded contributed the vaks of devotion and wisdom to the Kashmiri language
Zoon used to sing in the shade of a chinar tree
Habba Khatun introduced
to Kashmiri poetry,
is more or less equivalent to the English 'lyric'
Habba Khatun kept Yusuph Shah under her control
The songs of Habba Khatun are full of the sorrow of separation
into tho Kashmiri (language)
Gulam Ahmad 'Mahjoor'
(1885-1952) Gulam Ahmad 'Mahjoor'
Mahjoor has a place of honor among the poets of Kashmir
Mahjoor's real name was Ghulam Ahmad
Mahjoor worked as a
in Kashmir
Mahjoor was sixty-seven years old when he passed away in 1952
Zinda Koul 'Masterji'
(1884-1965) Zinda Koul 'Masterji'
Pandit Zinda Koul is a well-known poet of Kashmir
In the beginning 'Masterji' did not write in only Kashmiri
His well-known book in Kashmiri is Samran
Masterji had to face many difficulties in his life
Masterji started writing in Kashmiri in 1942
(Compulsion).
Masterji composed poetry only for (his own) pleasure
Dina Nath 'Nadim'
(March, 1916 - April 8, 1988) Dina Nath 'Nadim'
The death of Mahjoor and Masterji closed one phase of Kashmiri poetry
Nadim grew up in poverty
Nadim pursued his studies in great poverty and hardship
The following is illustrative: Burn and burn like a colorful field of
! Roar and roar like a waterfall! You are fire A furious fire of burning youth Come out And cross the hills and dales Raise a st.mp3! Be a st.mp3!
Another specimen is: Why should the share of a laborer be taken by a capitalist? Why should a honey bee circle the flowers and take away their honey?
Nadim introduced various poetic styles into Kashmiri
"I Shall Not Sing Today", is a good example of it
Nadim has used the Kashmiri language in his poetry with great grace and craftsmanship
"The Song of a Boatwoman from Dal Lake"
The following is another example: A lost stray cloud Floating aimlessly with the moon As if a beggar woman holds a leftover lump of watery rice In the corner of her headcover.
Nadim has also composed poetry in the folkstyle
For several years Nadim taught at the Hindu High School
Nadim has been greatly influenced by communism and by progressive writers
"The Bumblebee and the Narcissus".
Nadim has greatly influenced the young Kashmiri poets of today

Holy books will disappear, and then only the mystic f.mp3ula will remain

title: A Song by Habba Khatun" width="187" height="90">

entries.english: A Song by Habba Khatun A Song by Habba Khatun
Which rival of mine has lured you away from me? Why are you cross with me? Forget the anger and the sulkiness, You are my only love, Why are you cross with me?
My garden has blossomed into colorful flowers, Why are you away from me? My love, my only love, I think only of you, Why are you cross with me?
I kept my doors open half the night, Come and enter my door, my jewel, Why have you forsaken the path to my house? Why are you cross with me?
I swear, my love, I am waiting for you, dressed in colorful robes, My youth is in full bloom now, Why are you cross with me?
Oh, marksman, my bosom is open To the darts you throw at me
I have been wasting away like snow in summer heat. my youth is in its bloom
I have sought you over hills and dales, I have sought you from dawn till dusk, I have cooked dainty dishes for you
I shed incessant tears for you, I am pining for you, What is my fault, O, my love? Why don't you seek me out? Why are you cross with me?
The shock of your desertion has come as a blow to me, O cruel one, I continue to nurse the pain
I have not complained even to the spring breeze That is my agony
I swear by you I do not go out at all, I don't even show up at the spring
My hurt is marrow deep; I did not complain
I, Habba Khatun, am grieving now

title: Compulsion by Zinda Koul 'Masterji'" Compulsion" by Zinda Koul 'Masterji'" width="204" height="95">

entries.english: "Compulsion" by Zinda Koul 'Masterji' Compulsion by Zinda Koul 'Masterji'
One would cry and not restrain the tears, But crying is of no avail, Shedding incessant tears is of no avail, And knocking one's head against boulders is of no avail
The body is consumed minute by minute, suppressed by hunger and thirst and cold, chained by ailments and kith and kin depressed by constant worries and woes
Our ears have heard, Our hearts have believed, that sometime, somewhere, someone caught a distant glimpse of Him
I ask The one who is hidden far and away, The one who gives us a deaf ear, Does he ever enquire how we are? Does he ever recall where we are? Does he ever ask himself, "I wonder what is the lot o
We could argue, "Why expect love from the loveless? Why expect fruit from a willow? If you do not know his whereabouts, How can you plan his search?" But heart will not retract the steps For
It is the sound from within; It is like the fragrance of the musk

Mere compulsion! Mere helplessness! Mere affliction of desire and body! Indeed love is a painful obsession! Indeed beauty has no compassion! Love is not a child's play! Beauty is not mere enchantment!

title: The Song of a Boatman from Dal Lake by Dina Nath 'Nadim'

entries.english: "The Song of a Boatman from Dal Lake" by Dina Nath 'Nadim' The Song of a Boatman from Dal Lake by Dina Nath 'Nadim'
I got these crisp and fresh from the Dal hay valay, come and buy! hay valay, come and buy! These are tiny eggplants, and these are round gourds,
come and buy!
come and buy!
These are peppers, and these are brinjals
come and buy!
come and buy!
The crisp bundles of radishes are glittering in the shade of weeds, The red marsh turnip is blushing like a blushing beauty as if the dawn has blossomed into flowers.
come and buy!
come and buy!
May dust fall on you! Stop it! You have taken enough now
come and buy!
come and buy!
What can I tell you, dear lady, My child was born only last Thursday
come and buy!
come and buy!
My little one! My little one is pale like a radish, My little one is pale like jasmine, My little one is naked and nude, shivering and cold like a lump of ice
come and buy!
come and buy!
My little one's nose is like a lotus seed, Just like his father's nose; My little one's face is tiny, just like his mother's face
come and buy!
come and buy!
Lo! I seem to hear a baby cry; Lo! I seem to feel a sensation in my breast
come and buy!
